Diagnosis:
Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates severe dilation of the left ventricle with global hypokinesis. The left ventricular end-diastolic volume index is significantly elevated at 138 mL/m², and the left ventricular ejection fraction is severely reduced at 28%. Myocardial delayed enhancement sequences reveal a distinct, mid-wall pattern of late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) within the basal and mid-interventricular septum, sparing the subendocardium. Right ventricular volumes and systolic function are preserved with an ejection fraction of 52%, and no significant valvular abnormalities are identified.

Impression: Severe left ventricular dilation and systolic dysfunction, highly consistent with a non-ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM). The presence of mid-wall septal late gadolinium enhancement supports a non-ischemic etiology, which may carry prognostic significance for arrhythmic risk.

MRI of the brain demonstrates a large, heterogeneously enhancing mass centered in the right temporal lobe, measuring approximately 4.2 x 3.8 x 3.5 cm. The lesion is surrounded by an extensive zone of vasogenic edema, producing a significant mass effect with a 6 mm leftward midline shift and subfalcine herniation. There are central non-enhancing areas consistent with necrosis, as well as peripheral nodular enhancement.

Impression: Large, necrotic right temporal lobe mass with extensive surrounding vasogenic edema and midline shift, highly suspicious for a high-grade glioma such as glioblastoma multiforme. Urgent neurosurgical evaluation is recommended.

Fetal MRI performed at 29 weeks gestation demonstrates normal fetal intracranial anatomy and brain development. The cerebral hemispheres show symmetric and age-appropriate sulcation patterns, with normal opercularization. The lateral ventricular atria are within normal limits, measuring 5.8 mm on the left and 6.1 mm on the right. The corpus callosum is fully formed and visualized in its entirety, and the posterior fossa structures, including the cerebellum and brainstem, appear structurally normal.

Fetal thoracic, abdominal, and spinal structures are grossly intact with no signs of congenital diaphragmatic hernia or masses. Impression: Normal fetal MRI demonstrating normal fetal brain development and visceral anatomy for 29 weeks gestation.

Fetal MRI performed at 31 weeks gestation demonstrates complete agenesis of the corpus callosum. There is characteristic colpocephaly with dilation of the occipital horns of the lateral ventricles, with the atrial width measuring 12.5 mm on the left and 11.8 mm on the right, indicating moderate ventriculomegaly. A radial arrangement of the cerebral sulci pointing toward the third ventricle is observed, consistent with the absent corpus callosum. The third ventricle is superiorly displaced and dilated.

Impression: Complete agenesis of the corpus callosum with associated moderate bilateral ventriculomegaly and characteristic colpocephaly. No other intracranial or extra-axial abnormalities are identified; the posterior fossa structures and fetal spine appear intact for gestational age.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain was performed using multiplanar, multisequence protocols before and after the administration of intravenous contrast. There is no evidence of intracranial mass, acute ischemia, or hemorrhage. Ventricles, sulci, and extra-axial spaces are within normal limits for the patient's age. The major intracranial flow voids are intact, and there is no abnormal parenchymal or meningeal enhancement.

Impression: Unremarkable MRI of the brain. No structural abnormality, mass effect, or pathological signal alteration is identified to explain the clinical history of atypical migraines.

Cardiac MRI was performed utilizing steady-state free precession (SSFP) cine sequences, T1/T2 mapping, and late gadolinium enhancement (LGE). Left ventricular size, wall thickness, and mass are within normal limits, with an estimated ejection fraction (LVEF) of 62% and preserved global and segmental wall motion. The right ventricle is normal in volume and function with an ejection fraction of 56%. There is no evidence of myocardial edema on T2-weighted imaging, and LGE sequences show no abnormal myocardial scar or fibrosis.

Impression: Completely normal cardiac MRI study. Left and right ventricular chambers, systolic function, and myocardial tissue characteristics are within physiological limits, with no evidence of ischemic or non-ischemic injury, active myocarditis, or infiltrative disease.
